en.extract:
The black-and-chestnut warbling finch (Poospiza whitii) is a species of bird in the tanager family Thraupidae. It is found on the slopes of the Andes in western Argentina and western Bolivia. Its natural habitats are woodland borders and hedgerows.


Taxonomy

The black-and-chestnut warbling finch was formally described in 1883 by the English zoologist Philip Sclater under the current binomial name Poospiza whitii. The specific epithet was chosen to honour Ernest William White who had collected the specimen. The type locality is Cosquín, Córdoba in Argentina.
This species was formerly considered conspecific with the black-and-rufous warbling finch. The taxa were split based on molecular genetic and phenotypic data. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.

The '''black-and-chestnut warbling finch''' ('''''Poospiza whitii''''') is a species of bird in the tanager family [[Thraupidae]]. It is found on the slopes of the Andes in western Argentina and western Bolivia. Its natural habitats are woodland borders and hedgerows.

==Taxonomy==
[[File:PoospizaWhitiiSmit (cropped).jpg|thumb|left|220px]]
The black-and-chestnut warbling finch was [[species description|formally described]] in 1883 by the English zoologist [[Philip Sclater]] under the current [[binomial name]] ''Poospiza whitii''.<ref name=sclater>{{ cite journal | last=Sclater | first=Philip Lutley| author-link=Philip Sclater | year=1883 | title=Notes on the birds of the Argentine Republic | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | pages=37–43 [43, Plate IX]  | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/28679815 }}</ref> The specific epithet was chosen to honour Ernest William White who had collected the specimen.<ref name=sclater/><ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=407 }}</ref> The [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] is [[Cosquín, Córdoba]] in Argentina.<ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1970 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=XIII: Emberizinae, Catamblyrhynchinae, Cardinalinae, Thraupinae, Tersininae | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=119 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14483354 }}</ref>

This species was formerly considered [[conspecific]] with the [[black-and-rufous warbling finch]]. The taxa were split based on molecular genetic and phenotypic data.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Shultz | first1=Allison J. | last2=Burns | first2=Kevin J. | date=2013 | title=Plumage evolution in relation to light environment in a novel clade of Neotropical tanagers | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=66 | issue=1 | pages=112–125 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2012.09.011| pmid=23026808 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Jordan | first1=E.A. | last2=Areta | first2=J.I. | last3=Holzmann | first3=I. | date=2017 | title=Mate recognition systems and species limits in a warbling-finch complex (''Poospiza nigrorufa/whitii'') | journal=Emu | volume=117 | issue=4 | pages=344–358 | doi=10.1080/01584197.2017.1360746| s2cid=90815844 | hdl=11336/49976 | hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=July 2020 | title=Tanagers and allies | work=IOC World Bird List Version 10.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/tanagers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=27 October 2020 }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc/>
